title: TBM 380: “But We Have To Try Something!”
questions: Why do organizations keep returning to the same outcomes even after big change initiatives?
How can leaders communicate systems or complexity insights without triggering narrative fallacy?
What does it mean to co-author the narrative around change, and how do you do it in practice?
How can teams make risks visible earlier and commitments more reliable without scapegoating or simplistic fixes?
search_terms: work in progress reduce WIP narrative
summary: The post argues that organizational change often fails because leaders and teams default to narrative fallacies—neat stories that feel coherent but don’t alter the underlying attractor states. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who want to apply systems/complexity thinking while still providing actionable plans and a compelling action-to-outcome narrative. The example shows reframing “predictability” into practices that make risks visible earlier and commitments more reliable, creating space for complexity-aware experimentation.

title: TBM 286: Narrative Fallacies and the 2024 Narrative Soup
questions: Why do corporate explanations for inefficiency feel compelling but turn out to be wrong?
What causes 'narrative soup' and narrative camps inside large organizations?
How do layoffs and asymmetric pressures affect unity of purpose and corporate transformation stories?
What should an individual do when public narratives become their internal narratives?
search_terms: why corporate transformation stories feel like myths
narratives camps and identity at work
time wasted waiting on salaried people
fragile explanations when confronted with reality
summary: The post argues that people explain organizational inefficiency through narrative fallacies—emotionally satisfying stories that can become identity anchors and fuel conflict. It is aimed at leaders and knowledge workers trying to make sense of corporate transformation and discourse in 2024’s divisive environment, where clashing stakeholder narratives create a 'narrative soup.' The recommended response is self-reflection and safer ways to seek support and diverse perspectives.

title: TBM 253: Don't Fix Things. Write a New Story
questions: Why does aligning on the problem definition backfire in organizations?
How do competing narratives create factional conflict and oversimplification of complex issues?
What can leaders do to break a narrative stalemate without relying on an external existential threat?
How can a positive organizational narrative help people row in the same direction?
search_terms: sociotechnical systems no single root cause
how to craft a shared story at work
stop fixing things write a new story
summary: The post argues that organizational “problem alignment” is often counterproductive because people interpret what’s broken through different narratives shaped by their worldview and incentives. It explains how competing authority-backed narratives can produce a narrative stalemate where conflict replaces problem solving, and proposes that leaders should craft a new, positive story that enables shared action. The guidance is aimed at leaders and change agents trying to move teams forward amid persistent disagreement.

title: TBM 22/53: Attacking X vs. Starting Y
questions: How do we stop success theater without escalating conflict?
What should we replace an 'optics and smoke and mirrors' narrative with?
Are we getting dragged down by what we are trying to stop?
Where can we focus on a better, healthier narrative that people will embrace?
search_terms: we go where we look attention
plant and grow a better narrative
summary: This post addresses how to respond to “success theater” in teams—performative celebrations and optics that feel shallow or misleading. It’s for leaders and change agents who want to reduce that behavior by redirecting attention and replacing it with a more authentic narrative that delivers momentum, recognition, and closure in a deeper way.

title: TBM 15/53: Why Can't This Be Like...
questions: Why can’t our team execute like a past “successful” project?
How do we deal with amnesia or selective memory about what enabled earlier wins?
What should leaders do when people are stuck in old narratives and blame stories?
How can we create a new narrative that restores urgency and delivery?
search_terms: why can't we replicate past success
summary: The post explores why teams often fail to recreate the conditions of a past “heroic” project, arguing that fixed narratives about earlier success create narrative inertia. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who need practical ways to restore execution by changing the environment (cadence, access, autonomy, and blocker removal) so a new, compelling story can emerge.
